content.text: React has two closely related but distinct concepts that are easy to conflate: a
. Getting this distinction right unlocks a deeper understanding of how React works.
A JavaScript function (or class) that accepts props and returns JSX (or null). It is a live, reusable factory for producing UI descriptions.

A plain JavaScript object that describes what should be rendered — its type, props, key, ref, and children. It is the output of calling a component (or writing JSX). Elements are static, immutable descriptions.
is what the factory produces. React calls the factory during the render phase to get elements, then compares elements across renders to decide what to update.

attrs.alt: Diagram comparing a React element (plain object) with a React component (function that produces elements)

content.text: A Component Is a Function (or Class)
The simplest possible component is a JavaScript function that takes props and returns JSX:
The component is not HTML, not an instance, not a snapshot — it is a
that React can call. React calls it during the render phase with the current props and receives back elements (JSX).
Class components are also supported but less common since hooks:
An Element Is a Plain Object
When you write JSX or call
, you get a React element back. It is nothing more than a JavaScript object:
— the function. The element does not contain the output of calling Greeting; it contains a
of what to render. React will call
React follows a clear sequence from component definition to DOM update:
You define a component (a function or class). This happens once, at module load time.
When React needs to render, it calls your component with the current props. This produces elements (JSX).
React compares the new element tree to the previous one (reconciliation). It finds the differences.
React commits the differences to the DOM (mutation phase). Only changed nodes are updated.
Elements Are Cheap; Components Can Be Expensive
) is extremely cheap — it allocates a tiny plain object. The component function is not called at this point.
the component function: running the function body, executing all the hooks, creating child elements. Memoization with
prevents React from calling the component function again when props haven't changed.
React's reconciler compares elements from consecutive renders by checking the
fields. This is how React knows whether to update an existing component instance or create a new one:
This is the element-vs-component distinction in action: React compares
fields) to decide what to do with
A subtle distinction with real consequences: storing a component reference and storing an element reference behave very differently:
Defining a component inside another component is one of the most common React bugs. The inner function is a new object on every outer render, so the element's
field changes on every render, causing React to unmount and remount the inner component — destroying all its state.
Elements are plain objects and can be passed as props. This enables the children pattern and the slot pattern:
When you pass JSX as a prop, the element is created (the object is allocated) at the call site — not when the
receives a plain object; React renders the element when
function checks whether a value is a React element. It returns true for objects created by JSX or
is a lowercase string are called
— they map to native DOM or platform elements. Elements whose
is a function or class are
Host element — maps to a DOM node
Custom component — React calls the function
Fragment — renders children with no wrapper DOM
Suspense boundary — shows fallback while suspended
Lazy-loaded component — element triggers dynamic import

During the commit phase, React processes host elements by directly creating or updating DOM nodes. For custom elements, React calls the component function, processes the returned element tree, and continues recursively until only host elements remain.
is a function (or class) that accepts props and returns JSX — it is a live value you can pass around and call. A
is the plain object returned from JSX or

— it is an immutable description of what to render at a point in time. React calls components during rendering to produce elements, then compares consecutive element trees (reconciliation) to compute minimal DOM updates. The element's

field controls component identity — a stable type means React reuses an instance; a changed type means React remounts. Elements are cheap to create; the cost is in calling components and running hooks.
Element Trees and the Virtual DOM

When you call a component, it returns elements that may themselves reference other components. React recursively calls components until every element in the tree has a string type (host element). This fully-expanded tree of host elements is informally called the
— a description of what the real DOM should look like.
This final tree of string-typed elements is what the reconciler compares with the previous render to compute the minimal set of DOM mutations.
Render Props: Components That Return Elements
Because elements are plain objects and components are functions, you can pass a component (or any function that returns an element) as a prop — this is the

is not a component (it is not registered with React, has no fiber), but it is a plain function that produces elements. React simply calls it and uses the returned element. This pattern is less common today — hooks usually provide a cleaner alternative — but it remains powerful for certain abstractions.
Because components are JavaScript values, they form closures over their defining scope. A component defined inside another function has access to that function's variables:
the rendering component. They are stable references — React sees the same
on every render and does not remount.
Higher-Order Components: Functions That Return Components
is a function that takes a component and returns a new component. It illustrates the distinction: the input and output are both components (functions), not elements:
HOCs are components returned by calling a function. They must be defined outside render — creating a new HOC inside a render function would produce a different
Lazy Components: Elements That Trigger Code Splitting
wraps a dynamic import and returns a component that React can render lazily. The lazy component is used exactly like a regular component in elements:

The lazy wrapper is a component in every meaningful sense — it has a stable reference, React manages its lifecycle, and it participates in reconciliation normally. The only difference is that its first render may suspend while the module loads.

Component = factory function. Element = plain object description (output of JSX). React calls components during render; elements tell React what to call and with what props. Same type at same position = state preserved. Different type = remount. Never define components inside other components — this creates a new type reference each render, forcing a remount.
